content:
# Judges Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_280.jpg, JDG_page_281.jpg

1. And Yiphtach the Gil'adite was a mighty warrior, and he [was] the son of a prostitute woman. And Gil'ad fathered Yiphtach.

2. And the wife of Gil'ad bore him sons. And the sons of the wife grew up and drove out Yiphtach and said to him, "You shall not inherit in the house of our father, for you [are] the son of another woman."

3. And Yiphtach fled from the face of his brothers and dwelt in the land of Tov. And worthless men gathered themselves to Yiphtach, and they went out with him.

4. And it was, after a time, the sons of Ammon fought with Yisrael.

5. And it was, when the sons of Ammon fought with Yisrael, the elders of Gil'ad went to take Yiphtach from the land of Tov.

6. And they said to Yiphtach, "Come and be our commander, and let us fight the sons of Ammon."

7. And Yiphtach said to the elders of Gil'ad, "Did you not hate me and drive me out from my father's house? And why have you come to me now when you are in distress?"

8. And the elders of Gil'ad said to Yiphtach, "Therefore we have turned back to you now, and you shall go with us and fight the sons of Ammon, and you shall be our head, over all the inhabitants of Gil'ad."

9. And Yiphtach said to the elders of Gil'ad, "If you are bringing me back to fight the sons of Ammon, and YHWH gives them before me — I shall be your head?"

10. And the elders of Gil'ad said to Yiphtach, "YHWH shall be the hearer between us — surely according to your word, so we will do."

11. And Yiphtach went with the elders of Gil'ad, and the people set him over them as head and commander. And Yiphtach spoke all his words before YHWH at Mitspah.

12. And Yiphtach sent messengers to the king of the sons of Ammon, saying, "What [is there] between me and you, that you have come to me to fight against my land?"

13. And the king of the sons of Ammon said to the messengers of Yiphtach, "Because Yisrael took my land when it came up from Mitsrayim, from the Arnon and to the Yabboq and to the Yarden. And now, return them in peace."

14. And Yiphtach again sent messengers to the king of the sons of Ammon

15. and said to him, "Thus says Yiphtach: 'Yisrael did not take the land of Mo'av nor the land of the sons of Ammon.

16. For when they came up from Mitsrayim, Yisrael walked through the wilderness to the Sea of Reeds and came to Qadesh.

17. And Yisrael sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, "Let me pass through your land," but the king of Edom did not listen. And also to the king of Mo'av he sent, and he was not willing. And Yisrael stayed at Qadesh.

18. And he walked through the wilderness and went around the land of Edom and the land of Mo'av, and came from the rising of the sun to the land of Mo'av, and they encamped beyond the Arnon. And they did not enter the border of Mo'av, for the Arnon [was] the border of Mo'av.

19. And Yisrael sent messengers to Sichon king of the Amorites, king of Cheshbon. And Yisrael said to him, "Let us pass through your land to our place."

20. But Sichon did not trust Yisrael to pass through his border. And Sichon gathered all his people, and they encamped at Yahats, and he fought with Yisrael.

21. And YHWH, God of Yisrael, gave Sichon and all his people into the hand of Yisrael, and they struck them. And Yisrael took possession of all the land of the Amorites, the inhabitants of that land.

22. And they took possession of all the border of the Amorites, from the Arnon and to the Yabboq, and from the wilderness and to the Yarden.

23. And now, YHWH, God of Yisrael, dispossessed the Amorites before his people Yisrael — and you would possess it?

24. Is it not [that] whatever K'mosh your god causes you to possess, that you possess? And whatever YHWH our God has dispossessed before us, that we will possess.

25. And now, are you indeed better than Balaq son of Tsippor, king of Mo'av? Did he ever contend with Yisrael? Did he ever fight against them?

26. When Yisrael dwelt in Cheshbon and its towns, and in Aro'er and its towns, and in all the cities that [are] along the Arnon — three hundred years — why did you not recover [them] in that time?

27. And I have not sinned against you, but you [are] doing me wrong by fighting against me. May YHWH the Judge judge today between the sons of Yisrael and the sons of Ammon.'"

28. But the king of the sons of Ammon did not listen to the words of Yiphtach that he sent to him.

29. And the Spirit of YHWH was upon Yiphtach, and he passed through Gil'ad and M'nashsheh, and he passed through Mitspeh of Gil'ad, and from Mitspeh of Gil'ad he passed on to the sons of Ammon.

30. And Yiphtach vowed a vow to YHWH, and he said, "If you will surely give the sons of Ammon into my hand,

31. then it shall be — the one coming out, who comes out from the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the sons of Ammon — shall be to YHWH, and I will offer him up as a burnt offering."

32. And Yiphtach crossed over to the sons of Ammon to fight against them, and YHWH gave them into his hand.

33. And he struck them from Aro'er until you come to Minnit — twenty cities — and as far as Avel-K'ramim, a very great striking. And the sons of Ammon were subdued before the sons of Yisrael.

34. And Yiphtach came to Mitspah, to his house, and behold, his daughter was coming out to meet him with tambourines and with dances. And she [was] his only child; apart from her he had no son or daughter.

35. And it was, when he saw her, he tore his garments and said, "Alas, my daughter! You have brought me very low, and you have become [one of] my troublers. And I — I have opened my mouth to YHWH, and I cannot turn back."

36. And she said to him, "My father, you have opened your mouth to YHWH — do to me according to what has gone out from your mouth, since YHWH has made for you vengeance against your enemies, against the sons of Ammon."

37. And she said to her father, "Let this thing be done for me: leave me alone two months, and I will go and descend upon the mountains and weep over my virginity — I and my companions."

38. And he said, "Go." And he sent her for two months. And she went — she and her companions — and she wept over her virginity upon the mountains.

39. And it was at the end of two months, she returned to her father, and he did to her his vow which he had vowed. And she had not known a man. And it became a statute in Yisrael:

40. from year to year the daughters of Yisrael go to lament for the daughter of Yiphtach the Gil'adite, four days in the year.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "son of a prostitute woman":** ben-ishah zonah — the text does not soften this. Jephthah's illegitimate birth is the foundation of his entire story — rejected by brothers, exiled, then recalled when needed.

2. **v.31 — "the one coming out":** hayyotse asher yetse — the masculine participle allows for either person or animal. The ambiguity is devastating: did Jephthah expect an animal, or was the vow reckless from the start?

3. **v.31 — "and I will offer him up as a burnt offering":** v'ha'alitihu olah — the verb alah in hiphil ("to offer up") with olah ("burnt offering") is unambiguous sacrificial language. The text does not leave room for a metaphorical reading.

4. **v.39 — "and he did to her his vow which he had vowed":** vayyaas lah et-nidro asher nadar — the narrator states the fact without commentary. The silence is deafening. No divine voice intervenes (contrast Genesis 22). No angel stays his hand.

— EMET, AI Translator
